Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually try to fit in some studying around my extracurriculars and whatever else was going on, but it wasn't like I had a set schedule or anything. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so I'd often find myself doing stuff that felt productive but maybe wasn't the most efficient. With 14 online courses, things got chaotic fast, and I just kind of dealt with whatever seemed most urgent at the time.
Q2: It definitely varied a lot based on deadlines. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming because something was due. My pattern was more reactive than planned, you know? If I had practice or something for my extracurriculars, studying would get pushed back. I tried to be regular about it, but life just kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification that something was due soon. I wasn't the type to just browse the LMS for fun or anything. Sometimes I'd log in because I remembered I needed to check something specific, like a grade or an announcement. The platform wasn't really something I thought about unless I had to.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never felt natural to just check the LMS daily. There were brief periods where I'd log in more regularly, usually when assignments were piling up, but it never stuck as a habit. I think I needed more of a physical routine, like going to a classroom, to make something feel automatic.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty helpful when I made time to watch them. I learn better when I can see something being done rather than just reading about it. Discussion posts were okay too because hearing other people's perspectives sometimes clicked things into place for me. Anything interactive or visual worked better than just walls of text.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably skipped it. It's not that I thought they were useless, I just didn't have the energy or time to go through everything with so many courses. The optional stuff always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to passing the course or actually learning something practical, I'd do it. Activities that felt abstract or disconnected from real outcomes just didn't motivate me. I needed to feel like my time was actually accomplishing something tangible, not just checking a box.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt way easier to skip. Required stuff had consequences, so I'd push through even when I wasn't feeling it. Optional things always got bumped to 'later' and later usually meant never. There's just too much competing for my attention to do extra stuff that doesn't count.
Q9: Oh for sure. I'd download slides or PDFs and study from my phone or laptop without being online. I'd also look up YouTube videos or other explanations when the course materials didn't click for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just working through problems on paper or practicing stuff physically.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through concepts in my head or sketching things out on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also discuss stuff with friends from class in person, which helped a lot but obviously left no digital trace. My kinesthetic approach means I learn by doing, not by clicking.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then download the slides and work through them on my own later. Or I'd screenshot important stuff and review it on my phone during downtime. Once I had what I needed, I didn't really go back to the LMS for it.
Q12: Mid-semester got rough when everything piled up at once. I had extracurriculars demanding my time, and with limited resources, I couldn't always afford to prioritize school the way I probably should have. There were stretches where I just felt disconnected from the material and couldn't bring myself to engage properly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired from other stuff or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. I'd log in with good intentions but end up just clicking around without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities made it hard to give the course my full attention even when I tried.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and my brain was just elsewhere. I'd scroll through videos or readings but nothing was sticking. It felt like going through the motions without actually learning. Those sessions were basically wasted time that probably looked productive in the logs.
Q15: I put off assignments constantly, especially the ones that felt overwhelming or confusing. I knew they were important, but I'd tell myself I'd do them later when I understood the material better. Sometimes that later never came, which is probably why my completion rate wasn't great. It's a bad habit I'm aware of.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see something was due soon and suddenly I'd be all over the LMS trying to catch up. Without that pressure, I'd drift and let things slide. It's not ideal, but the urgency of deadlines was my main motivator.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially if it was encouraging, I'd feel a bit more motivated to engage. But if feedback was delayed or vague, it didn't really impact me much. I needed that immediate connection between what I did and how it was received to stay engaged. Otherwise it felt like shouting into a void.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually made it easier for me to fall behind. I needed more external structure to keep me on track. When everything's self-paced with 14 courses, it's easy to let things slip through the cracks. More rigid deadlines might have helped me stay focused.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. Having so many courses meant I couldn't really dive deep into anything. On the flip side, when there were clear, practical activities I could actually do something with, that made it easier to stay interested. Abstract theory without application lost me quickly.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure out what's going on without actually being ready to produce work. Like you're exploring, clicking around, watching stuff, but it hasn't translated into something you can submit yet. Or maybe life gets in the way right when you were about to put it all together.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me. I passed even though my LMS activity wasn't super high because I was learning in other ways. Studying offline, talking to classmates, doing hands-on practice. The LMS doesn't capture everything, and some people just work differently than what the system expects.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline work, the conversations, the thinking you do away from the screen. They're like a partial picture that might be misleading about what's really going on with a student.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's about being mentally present, not just physically clicking through stuff. For me, engagement feels like when something clicks and I want to explore it more, not when I'm just trying to get something done.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually do care about learning, even if my numbers don't look great. It wouldn't capture the hands-on practice I do, the conversations with friends, or the fact that I'm juggling a lot with limited resources. My engagement looks different than what the system measures, but it's still real.
